explanation: The natural language sentence describes a requirement that once the controller enters fail-safe mode, it must not leave this mode (i.e., must not transition to any other mode) unless a power cycle occurs (the machine is turned off and then on). To encode this, let failSafeMode be the atomic proposition indicating that the controller is in fail-safe mode, and off indicate that the machine is completely powered off. The property says: if failSafeMode becomes true, then from that point on, it stays true (controller is locked in fail-safe mode) until a power cycle is performed. A power cycle can be represented by the occurrence of 'off' (system off) followed by the system being turned back on (i.e., not off). To be precise, we can say: After entering failSafeMode, it remains true until off becomes true. Formally, this is: G((failSafeMode & X(!failSafeMode)) -> F off) and G(failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off)). This means: 1) If the controller leaves failSafeMode, it must have observed off at some point in the future before leaving. 2) While in failSafeMode, it remains there until the system is off. The second formula captures the key property, since leaving failSafeMode (failSafeMode becomes false) is only allowed after off occurred.

explanation_dict.natural_language: failSafeMode is active
Controller cannot leave fail-safe mode unless the machine is powered off (off becomes true).
Globally, if failSafeMode is true, then it has to stay true until off becomes true (power cycle)

explanation: This sentence states that once the controller enters fail-safe mode, it cannot leave fail-safe mode unless there is a power cycle (power off and then on). We interpret 'being in fail-safe mode' as the proposition failSafeMode, and 'power cycle' as a sequence where the system goes off (off), and then is turned on again (not off). The formula must enforce that globally, once failSafeMode becomes true, it will remain true (the system is stuck there), unless interrupted by an occurrence of off. After being off, it is possible to exit failSafeMode. The translation uses the 'release' (R) operator dual as appropriate, or can be also formulated using 'until' and modal logic. Here, we use the implication: G(failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off)). This requires that as long as failSafeMode is true, it must stay true until off (power cycle) happens. Only then, the system is allowed not to be in failSafeMode. If the system never goes off, then once failSafeMode is entered, it must stay forever.

explanation_dict.natural_language: Controller is in fail-safe mode
'without a power cycle' → i.e., unless off occurs
Cannot exit fail-safe mode unless a power cycle occurs: Once failSafeMode is entered, it stays true until off occurs
Globally this invariant holds

explanation:
The requirement says that once the controller enters fail-safe mode, it cannot return to any other mode unless the machine has been powered off and then on again. This means: after 'failSafeMode' becomes true, the system must remain in fail-safe mode until a complete 'power cycle' occurs. We model 'power cycle' as the event where the system goes 'off' and then, at some later point, the 'powerButton' is pressed (as a proxy for 'turn on') and the system leaves the 'off' state. Thus: Once in failSafeMode, globally, the system must stay in failSafeMode until there is a subsequence where: first off holds (the machine is off), and then at some future point the system is on (i.e., !off). In LTL, we formalize this as: Globally, if failSafeMode becomes true, then (failSafeMode holds) UNTIL (off holds AND (eventually (!off))). The transition out of failSafeMode is only allowed after such a power cycle.

The LTL formula is: G( (failSafeMode) -> ((failSafeMode) U (off & F(!off))) )

explanation_dict.natural_language: failSafeMode becomes true
the system must stay in failSafeMode until power cycle (off then on)
if in failSafeMode, remain there until after power cycle
Globally, this must always hold

explanation:
We formalize the requirement that, once the controller enters fail-safe mode (failSafeMode becomes true), it cannot return to any other mode, unless there is a power cycle (the system is turned off, off, and then turned on again).

Step 1: Identify the atomic proposition representing fail-safe mode: 'failSafeMode'.
Step 2: 'Cannot return from fail-safe mode' means: as long as failSafeMode holds, it stays true (it is not possible to leave failSafeMode) — unless there is an intervening power cycle.
Step 3: A ‘power cycle’ consists of 'off' (system is off) occurring at any moment, after which the system is allowed to return to other modes.
Step 4: The behavior can be read as: between two occurrences of 'off', if the system ever enters failSafeMode, it must remain in failSafeMode until 'off' occurs.

This can be captured by requiring that: always, if we are not in the 'off' state, and failSafeMode has become true and no 'off' has occurred since, then failSafeMode remains true until 'off'.

This can be represented with the following formula:

G( (failSafeMode & (!off U X off)) -> (failSafeMode U off) )

But for clarity and adherence to LTL, a more precise reading using an invariant outside of power cycles is:

G( (failSafeMode & (!off W off)) -> (failSafeMode W off) )

However, since standard LTL does not have 'W' (weak until), we can use U and disjunction, or, more simply:

G( ( failSafeMode & ( !off U off ) ) -> ( failSafeMode U off ) )

Alternatively, the past can’t be referenced, so we have to say: Between two power cycles (off), once failSafeMode has been entered, it is maintained until the next occurrence of 'off'. This translates to:

G( (failSafeMode & !off) -> (failSafeMode U off) )

So, whenever the mode is fail-safe and the system is not off, failSafeMode must stay true at least until off.

explanation_dict.natural_language: Controller is in fail-safe mode
System is not off
failSafeMode stays true until off
Globally, whenever failSafeMode & !off, then failSafeMode U off

explanation: We want to formalize: The controller cannot return from fail-safe mode to any other mode without a power cycle. This means that once 'failSafeMode' is true, the system must stay in that mode until a power cycle (modeled by 'off' becoming true and then false again). Thus, whenever 'failSafeMode' becomes true, it remains true at every step until the system turns off ('off' becomes true). After 'off', only then can 'failSafeMode' change and possibly become false. In LTL, this is stated by: Globally, if failSafeMode is true, then it remains true until off becomes true: G(failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off)). This prohibits leaving failSafeMode except via a power cycle.

explanation_dict.natural_language: Cannot leave failSafeMode until power cycle (system turns off)
Always enforce this

explanation: The sentence specifies that once the controller is in fail-safe mode, it cannot transition out of fail-safe mode (i.e., to any other mode) unless there is a power cycle (off, then on again). In LTL, this means: Whenever failSafeMode becomes true, from that point forward, the system cannot leave failSafeMode (i.e., failSafeMode remains true) until it sees a power cycle. We model power cycle as the system going 'off' and then subsequently turning on (i.e., !off U (off & X(!off))). Therefore, we require: Whenever failSafeMode is entered, it must continuously stay true until a power cycle occurs. This can be captured as: G( failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U powerCycle) ), where powerCycle is defined as (off & X(!off)).

explanation_dict.natural_language: Entering 'fail-safe mode'
Cannot return from fail-safe mode to any other mode without a power cycle (off, then on).
explanation_dict.LTL: failSafeMode must hold until a power cycle (off & X(!off))

explanation_dict.natural_language: Power cycle: turning off, then turning on.
Whenever failSafeMode is entered, it stays active until a power cycle.

explanation:
This requirement means that once the system enters fail-safe mode (failSafeMode), it cannot leave fail-safe mode (i.e., failSafeMode will continue to be true) until a power cycle occurs (the system turns off and then on). Since there is no explicit 'mode' proposition, we abstract 'returning from fail-safe mode' as 'failSafeMode becomes false after being true', and a power cycle is the sequence of off (power down) followed by not off (power up). The formula enforces that after entering failSafeMode, it cannot become false again unless a power cycle intervenes.

To formalize:
- Once failSafeMode becomes true, it never becomes false again until a power cycle (off occurs and then the system turns on/off is false) happens.
- To capture this: Whenever failSafeMode is true now, if in the future it becomes false (at some point), then between now and that point, there must have been a power cycle event.

A way to encode 'cannot return from failSafeMode unless a power cycle' is:
Globally, if failSafeMode is true and in the future failSafeMode is false, then in between, there must be an occurrence of off followed by not off (that's a power cycle).

A classic LTL idiom for this is:
G((failSafeMode & F(!failSafeMode)) -> F(off & X(!off & F(!failSafeMode))))
But to strictly ensure that failSafeMode can only become false after a power cycle, we also ensure that once failSafeMode holds, it cannot become false until off has occurred, and after off occurs, it becomes not off (power comes back). So the full safe version is:

G((failSafeMode & X(!failSafeMode)) -> (F(off & X(!off & X(!failSafeMode)))))

But to ensure that, after failSafeMode is set, it stays true unless after a power cycle, we usually write:
G((failSafeMode & X(!failSafeMode)) -> (off & X(!off)))

But since failSafeMode may continue in the next steps, to summarize in a more general form:
G((failSafeMode & X(!failSafeMode)) -> (off & X(!off)))

That is, failSafeMode can only be cleared (i.e., change from true to false) in the step immediately after a power cycle occurred (off now, system turns power up next).

Alternative: To also cover if failSafeMode can become false at _any time_ after power cycle, we can ensure: any time failSafeMode transitions from true to false, it must be immediately after power cycle (off and then not off).
This is what the formula above encodes.

Therefore, the main ideas are:
- 'failSafeMode transitions from true to false' is (failSafeMode & X(!failSafeMode))
- 'power cycle just happened' is (off & X(!off))
- So, (failSafeMode & X(!failSafeMode)) -> (off & X(!off))
- Globally, this condition applies at all times.

explanation_dict.natural_language: failSafeMode transitions from true to false (controller attempts to leave fail-safe mode)
Power cycle occurs (off, then not off)
Cannot leave fail-safe mode except immediately after power cycle
Applies globally (at every moment in time)

explanation:
First, we identify the relevant atomic propositions from your dictionary:

failSafeMode: the controller is in fail-safe mode.
off: the system is completely de-energized (powered off).
powerButton: the power button is pressed (can indicate on or off, depending on how it's defined in the environment, but generally together with current power state gives a power cycle). However, as there is no explicit 'on' state, we consider a power cycle as the system being turned off and then turned back on. Strictly in LTL, it's difficult to refer to 'before X, after X' relationships using only LTL operators. However, the property requires that, once in failSafeMode, it is not possible to return to any other mode (i.e., exit failSafeMode) without first going through the powered-off state ('off').

So, the requirement: Once the system enters failSafeMode, it cannot leave failSafeMode again (i.e., failSafeMode becomes false) until the system is first in the 'off' state (i.e., off becomes true).

This is typically encoded as: 'Whenever we leave failSafeMode (failSafeMode becomes false after being true), it must be after having first visited the state off.'

Let us denote:
- (failSafeMode) -- controller is in fail-safe mode
- (off) -- system is powered off

The LTL formula can be constructed as: Whenever failSafeMode becomes true, then failSafeMode must remain true until off holds. This ensures you cannot leave failSafeMode before experiencing an 'off' state. So, globally, anytime failSafeMode goes true, it must remain true until off occurs. This is:

G (failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off))

This says: globally, if we are in failSafeMode, then (failSafeMode remains true) until off becomes true (power cycle).
If more strictness is desired (so that, after off, it's allowed to leave failSafeMode, but not before), this is precise for the given requirement.

The step-by-step reasoning is given in the explanation_dict below.

explanation_dict.natural_language: Once in fail-safe mode, the controller cannot return to any other mode until the machine is powered off.
explanation_dict.LTL: (failSafeMode) must stay true until (off) is true.

explanation_dict.natural_language: Globally, when failSafeMode is true, it must stay true until off is true.

explanation:
We want to formalize that: Once the controller enters fail-safe mode, it cannot leave fail-safe mode unless there is a power cycle (the system has been turned off and back on).

1. 'failSafeMode' is our atomic proposition that is True when system is in fail-safe mode.
2. 'off' represents power-off (part of a power cycle).

We need to state that from any time after failSafeMode becomes true, until we see an 'off', it is always failSafeMode. In other words, after entering failSafeMode, we cannot return to any other mode unless there is a power-off in between.

In LTL: Whenever we are in failSafeMode, then until we see 'off', we are always in failSafeMode. This is written: G(failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off)).

However, to strictly enforce that once failSafeMode holds, we remain in failSafeMode (no transitions to other modes) until a power cycle, we want a stronger property. In fact, after entering failSafeMode, only 'off' can end it; we should not see 'not failSafeMode' unless 'off' occurred.

So, globally, if we are in failSafeMode, then:
- In all subsequent states until 'off', failSafeMode remains true.

So: G(failSafeMode -> G(failSafeMode W off))
(where W is 'weak until', but as per allowed operators, we can use U and logical operators: 'phi W psi' = G(phi) | (phi U psi)).

Thus, G(failSafeMode -> (G(failSafeMode) | (failSafeMode U off)))

Alternatively, we can write: It is never the case that we exit failSafeMode (i.e., failSafeMode becomes false), before we have seen an 'off' since we entered failSafeMode.
So: G((failSafeMode & X(!failSafeMode)) -> F(off & !failSafeMode))
But this is more convoluted and less direct.

The most standard simple way is:
G(failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off))
That is, whenever in failSafeMode, it remains true until 'off' occurs.

That matches the desired semantics.

explanation_dict.natural_language: controller is in fail-safe mode
controller cannot return from fail-safe mode to any other mode until power cycle (off)
Globally, if controller is in fail-safe mode, it must remain until power cycle

explanation: We want to capture the fact that once the controller enters failSafeMode, it cannot return to any other mode unless a power cycle (turning off, then turning on) occurs. This means: whenever failSafeMode becomes true, it should stay true until a power cycle occurs. We can model a power cycle as the event where the system becomes 'off' (off=true) and then 'on' again (off=false). So, as long as there is no sequence where the system is turned off and then on, failSafeMode must continue to hold. Thus, globally, if failSafeMode becomes true, then it stays true until and unless we see a sequence where the system is 'off' and then on again. Let 'powerCycled' represent the occurrence of an 'off' followed by a step where 'off' is false (i.e., the system is ON after being OFF). We conservatively state: Whenever failSafeMode /is/ true, it cannot become false unless a power cycle occurs between leaving failSafeMode. Since we do not have a dedicated 'powerCycled' atomic, we'll encode it with LTL. The simplest and conservative representation: Globally, if failSafeMode is ever true, then either it remains true forever, or it can only become false after an 'off' occurs in the future. That is, whenever failSafeMode becomes true, it stays true until the system turns off. This captures that you can't exit failSafeMode without turning off the system.

explanation_dict.natural_language: Controller is in fail-safe mode
System is turned off (power cycle required)
Once failSafeMode is true, it is true until off
Globally, this holds at all times
